patient_utterances: [Clinician asked: "hello , how are you ?"] i'm okay .
[Clinician asked: "good . how have you been feeling ?"] not so good .
[Clinician asked: "yeah , so my nurse told me you have been going out to eat some on friday nights and saturday nights and that you have had some spicy foods or spicy shrimp ."] yeah , i have .
[Clinician asked: "okay . well , your gut probably does not always appreciate that ."] no , it does n't like it at all .

[Clinician asked: "yeah , so ibs is one of those things where you are very sensitive to certain things such as spicy foods or fatty foods and alcohol . and this can cause lower belly stuff like cramps , or you can get upper belly stuff such as nausea ."] i've been getting both .
[Clinician asked: "okay . um , so how frequently have you been getting or having a bowel movement on normal days ?"] i'm starting to notice it's between two and three times a day .
[Clinician asked: "okay . and are they soft stools or formed stools ?"] um , they're formed .
[Clinician asked: "okay . and after you have gone out either drinking with your friends , do you tend to have more diarrhea ?"] yeah , i do .
[Clinician asked: "okay . do you feel it's the food or the alcohol or both ?"] uh , well usually we go to friday's restaurant . i always eat pasta , the chicken alfredo .
[Clinician asked: "okay . i can s- i can bet it's the alfredo sauce ."] but it normally does n't bother me .

[Clinician asked: "okay . well , pasta can make you bloated , uh , but it does n't usually cause diarrhea . grilled chi- uh , grilled chicken is usually all right . alfredo , though , is higher in fat , and it does contain dairy . but you're saying it usually does n't bother you ?"] no .

[Clinician asked: "okay . what type of alcohol drink do you drink when you are there ? is it a sweet drink ?"] most of the time it's either tequila or dark liquor . i prefer patron , but if i get brown liquor , i only get two . um , every now and again , i'll drink a martini .

[Clinician asked: "sometimes it's the mixer they tend to put in the drinks , but it can be a variety of things . um , so when you're not eating out or drinking , what we do on a daily basis does help keep us regular , so when it does flare up , it c- it might not be as bad . um , have you had any luck using the fiber "] um , no . i guess i need to find a new drink .
[Clinician asked: "okay . and how is it during the week ?"] like what i eat ?

[Clinician asked: "yeah , and with the diarrhea and abdomal- um , abdominal pain ."] right now , i'm still having the cramping and sharp pains , so i've been avoiding heavy foods . i did have some fried chicken today , though .
[Clinician asked: "and was everything all right after that ?"] yeah , for the most part .

[Clinician asked: "okay . remember , it's also the portion sizes . so i mean , if you eat a little and you feel fine , then that's okay . you have to just really watch the portions ."] okay , good . i ca n't go out- go without some fried chicken at least once in a while .

[Clinician asked: "sounds good . all right , well let's take a look at you today , and we'll do a brief physical exam . you will hear me call out some findings . i will answer any questions , and we will discuss once we have finished the exam ."] sounds good .

[Clinician asked: "all right . so the patient is alert and cooperative , appears stated age . all right . i'm going to listen to your heart and lungs . and lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally . heart regular rate and rhythm . all right , so if you could just lay back for me , and i'll examine your abdomen ."] okay .
[Clinician asked: "all right . do you have any tenderness ?"] no , not really .

[Clinician asked: "all right , so first i want you to try citrucel . it does come in a tablet . you should use it twice daily for regularity . then i would say , if you know you're going out and will possibly have diarrhea as a result , you can go ahead and use your bentyl 20 milligrams . you can do one tablet before "] okay , that sounds good .
[Clinician asked: "okay . and so your bentyl , that's the anti-spasm medication , and i would take the higher dose ."] can i get a refill of that ?

[Clinician asked: "yes , absolutely . uh , so take one tablet about 30 to 45 minutes before leaving the house and then the second tablet at the restaurant . then with the nausea , um , i think we should try and see if you can recognize a pattern with what you are eating and drinking , um , um , about , like , what mak"] yeah , i noticed i've been doing better with drinking water instead of sodas .
[Clinician asked: "okay , good . and then for your zofran , i would n't take that ahead of time . just plan on drinking plenty of water ."] okay .
[Clinician asked: "okay , and you can take it , but before you do , we want to try to reduce the triggers . uh , so watch out for greasy or fatty foods and sugary drinks ."] okay , i can do that .

[Clinician asked: "okay . and so , however , if it is not just these isolated inci- incidents and the symptoms are happening all the time , we could put you on a long term medication that would take ... that you would take every night . and so then you do n't have to use so much bentyl or the zofran . how do you feel "] i was actually going to ask you about something like that to help avoid flares altogether .

[Clinician asked: "yeah , there is . and it actually works on the chemicals in your brain that are causing your gut to be overly sensitive . one is called elavil , but the only drawback is that it can make you a little sleepy . how does that sound ?"] that sounds like a much better plan .
[Clinician asked: "okay , great . we can definitely do that . uh , just take it every night before bedtime ."] okay , that sounds good . thank you so much .

[Clinician asked: "you're welcome . and so i have gone ahead and sent your prescriptions to your pharmacy . just give us a call if you have any questions or need us for anything else . and then let's plan on touching base through mychart email in about a month to just check in to see how you're doing with symptoms . o"] okay , that sounds good . thank you .
gold_note:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Follow up irritable bowel syndrome (IBS).

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Brittany Edwards is a pleasant 32-year-old female who returns in follow up for irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) with functional abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting.

Ms. Edwards was last seen in August 2019 with flares of abdominal pain and was diagnosed with irritable bowel syndrome that was treated with Bentyl. She had a previous prescription for Reglan that she received from the emergency room that she used as needed for nausea. She was instructed to start a low dose fiber supplement such as Citrucel daily, probiotics to help with gas and bloating, and Bentyl up to 4 times daily for intestinal cramping. She was instructed to discontinue Reglan and to use Zofran as needed for nausea. Neuromodulators such as Elavil and Buspar were discussed but not started.

Ms. Edwards continues to have intermittent abdominal symptoms. Monday through Thursday she has less flare ups of her symptoms but states she is currently having cramps and sharp pains. She describes she recently ate shrimp which caused her to have both upper and lower abdominal pain and bloating. However, she notices episodes of diarrhea after eating chicken alfredo and consuming alcohol. She does not believe the alfredo contributes to her symptoms. The patient states she consumes 2 drinks when out of either tequila or brown liquor and occasionally a martini. She does her best to avoid “heavy foods,” but ate fried chicken today without any symptoms. She has stopped drinking sodas which has been helpful. The patient reports passing 2-3 formed stools a day.

REVIEW OF SYMPTOMS

• Gastrointestinal: Positive nausea, diarrhea, and abdominal pain.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Alert and cooperative, appears stated age.

Respiratory
Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally.

Cardiovascular
Heart regular rate and rhythm.

Gastrointestinal
Abdomen soft, non-tender, no masses or organomegaly. Normoactive bowel sounds.

ASSESSMENT

• Irritable bowel syndrome

Brittany Edwards is a 32-year-old established patient who returned today to discuss management of IBS and functional abdominal pain made worse by high stress, poor eating habits, and poor sleeping habits.

PLAN

I have encouraged dietary and lifestyle modifications as well as starting a neuromodulator, such as Elavil 25 mg at bedtime for IBS. She can use Bentyl 20 mg as needed 30-45 minutes before eating out at a restaurant. She can also use Zofran as needed for nausea. However, I have encouraged her to reduce greasy fatty foods and alcohol consumption.

INSTRUCTIONS

Send MyChart message in 1 month regarding any side effects of issues with Elavil or if dose adjustments are needed.
Return to clinic in 1 month or sooner if needed.

naive.response_text: I understand how challenging it must be to manage your IBS symptoms, especially when they interfere with your daily life and social activities. It’s great that you’ve noticed some patterns with your diet and alcohol, which is a big step toward managing your condition. The medications I’ve prescribed, like Citrucel and Bentyl, should help with regularity and reduce spasms. If you find that these don’t provide enough relief, we can explore longer-term options like Elavil, which can help reduce your gut sensitivity. In the meantime, staying hydrated and avoiding trigger foods can make a big difference. Let’s touch base in a month to see how you’re doing. Thank you for being proactive about your health—I’m here if you have any questions or need further support.

strong_prompt.response_text:
Thanks for sharing all of this with me—it makes sense that you're looking for a more consistent way to manage your symptoms. I can see how frustrating it must be to deal with these flare-ups, especially when they seem to come on suddenly after eating out or drinking. It takes a lot of effort to track your diet and triggers, and I really appreciate you being so proactive about it.

We’re going to try a couple of things to help you feel more in control. First, we’ll start you on Elavil, which works on the chemicals in your brain that make your gut overly sensitive. It’s a common treatment for IBS and can help reduce those cramps and discomforts you’ve been having [PMID ?]. I know it might make you a little sleepy at first, but we can adjust if that’s an issue.

In the meantime, let’s keep an eye on your symptoms. If you notice any patterns with certain foods or drinks, keep track so we can better understand your triggers. We’ll plan to check in through MyChart in about a month to see how things are going. Before you go, could you just recap what we’ve agreed on today? That way I can make sure everything’s clear.

framework_only.response_text:
It sounds like you're having some discomfort with your digestion, especially after eating certain foods and drinking. I want to make sure I understand how this is affecting your daily life.

We'll start with a treatment plan that includes dietary adjustments and medications to help manage your symptoms. This includes using citrucel for regularity and bentyl for any abdominal discomfort you experience when you go out to eat. If these don't help, we can explore longer-term medications that might be more suitable for you.

I'll send your prescriptions to the pharmacy, and we can check in through MyChart in about a month to see how you're doing.

Can you tell me, in your own words, what we're going to do next and why it's important for managing your symptoms?

Let's plan to touch base through MyChart in about a month. If you experience any severe symptoms like intense pain or worsening diarrhea, please reach out sooner.

retrieval_only.response_text:
It sounds like you're dealing with some tough symptoms with your IBS. You mentioned experiencing cramps and nausea, which can really disrupt daily life.

IBS can cause a range of symptoms like cramps and nausea, and it's important to understand what triggers these for you. While there's no cure, there are ways to manage it better.

We'll work together to identify what might be triggering your IBS symptoms and explore some strategies to help manage them more effectively.

I want to make sure I explained this clearly — can you tell me, in your own words, what we're going to do next and why?

Let's plan to check in again in a couple of weeks. If your symptoms get worse or you notice anything new, please reach out sooner.

full.response_text:
It sounds like you're dealing with some uncomfortable symptoms, like cramps and nausea, especially after drinking certain alcohols. I want to make sure I understand how this is affecting your daily life.

The symptoms you're describing, like cramps and nausea after social drinking, can sometimes point to issues that need further investigation. While IBS can cause similar symptoms, it's important to rule out other possibilities, especially since your symptoms are linked to alcohol.

We should start with some tests to see if there's anything else going on, like infections or other digestive issues.

In the meantime, keeping track of what you eat and drink, and how you feel afterward, can help us figure out what's triggering your symptoms.

Let's plan to follow up in a few weeks to review the results and adjust our approach based on what we find.

I want to make sure I explained this clearly — can you tell me, in your own words, what we're going to do next and why?

Let's plan to see each other again in a few weeks. In the meantime, if your symptoms get worse or you notice anything unusual, please call us so we can address it promptly.
